content:
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="propinquus_1">
							, <gloss>kinsman</gloss>. It is not known what was the relationship of
							Tubero to Cicero. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Tubero_1">:
							Q. Aelius Tubero, the prosecutor, was the son of L. Aelius Tubero, the
							Pompeian commander, and was lying sick on board his father's ship at the
							time when Ligarius prevented the landing in Africa (see Introd., p. 2?5
							of text). Cicero throughout the speech conveys the impression that
							personal resentment was at the bottom of Q. Tubero's action in opposing
							the pardon of Ligarius. <milestone n="226.3" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Pansa_1">:
							C. Vibius, cos. <date when="-0043">B.C. 43</date> (see Phil. xiv), at
							this time a leading supporter of Caesar. He was a petitioner for
							Ligarius. <milestone n="226.5" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quo_vertam_1">
							, <gloss>which way to turn</gloss>. <milestone n="226.10" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="necessarius_1">:
							Cicero's <foreign xml:lang="lat">necessitudo</foreign> to Pansa appears
							to have consisted in their working together in behalf of Ligarius. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_esset_1">
							(obj. of <foreign xml:lang="lat">fecerit</foreign>), <gloss>that it is
							no longer a new case</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_parte_2">,
							i.e. the side of Pompey, on which the younger Tubero (the accuser) had
							been, though he had since become reconciled to Caesar. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Considio_2">:
							C. Considius Longus, a propraetor of Africa in <date when="-0050">B.C.
							50</date>, the year before the Civil War. <milestone n="226.21" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="socus_2">:
							see note on Verres 1, sect. <ref n="Cic. Ver. 1.1.13">13</ref> (<ref n="pl32.29">p. 32, l. 29</ref>). <milestone n="226.22" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="satis_facere_2">,
							etc.: if a governor left his province before the expiration of his term,
							he could appoint any officer he chose to govern. <foreign xml:lang="lat">pro
							praetore</foreign> in his place, and such a substitute exercised the <foreign xml:lang="lat">imperium</foreign> of his superior. It was usual,
							although not obligatory, to appoint the highest subordinate officer, the
							quaestor. Hence this apologetic expression of the orator: Ligarius, he
							says, was so highly esteemed both by the Roman residents and by the
							native provincials that Considius could do no less than appoint him. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qui_Africa_3">,
							i.e. the Roman citizens there. <milestone n="226.28" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cupiditate_inconsiderata_3">
							, <gloss>headlong partisanship</gloss>. <milestone n="226.29" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="salutis_3">
							and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="studi_3">
							limit <foreign xml:lang="lat">ducem</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="studi_3_2">, <gloss>partisan
							zeal</gloss>. <milestone n="226.30" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ducem_3">,
							i.e. they wished to organize, at first to secure their own safety, and
							afterwards to aid the Pompeian party, and hence they desired a military
							leader. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_3">: § <ref n="AG 546">546</ref>, a (325, b); B. 288, 2; G. 581; H. 600, i (52
							I, i); H.-B. 566, a. <milestone n="227.1" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="implicari_3">,
							i.e. refused to commit himself to any such action. <milestone n="227.2" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="praetor_3">,
							i.e. as <foreign xml:lang="lat">propraetor</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="obtinuerat_3">
							, <gloss>had held</gloss>, in some former year. Of course, therefore, he
							had no legitimate authority in Africa at the present time, for the <foreign xml:lang="lat">imperium</foreign> had to be conferred by a special
							and very definite act; hence the expression <foreign xml:lang="lat">si
							illud</foreign>, etc. (1.4). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qui_cuperet_4">
							, <gloss>being one who wished</gloss>. <milestone n="227.12" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_pacatissima_4">:
							Africa was one of the earliest and most thoroughly conquered of the
							provinces. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ita_gessit_4">,
							etc.: in contrast to others who welcomed a state of war to escape or
							hide the Consequences of their acts of violence or oppression. <milestone n="227.13" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pacem_esse_4">,
							subject of <foreign xml:lang="lat">expediret</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="profectio_4">
							, <gloss>his going there</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_5">, <gloss>that
							during which</gloss> (a forced use of the acc. of duration of time). <milestone n="227.21" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Uticae_5">:
							a Phoenician city in Africa, older than Carthage, under whose supremacy
							it was always restless. For this reason it helped Rome against Carthage,
							and was rewarded with the gift of territory. After Africa was made a
							Roman province, Utica was its capital. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="occurrat_6">:
							indir. question depending on <foreign xml:lang="lat">reformidat</foreign>
							. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="imperator_7">:
							after the news of Pompey's death (<date when="-0048">B.C. 48</date>)
							Caesar was made <foreign xml:lang="lat">dictator rei publicae
							constituendae</foreign>, at the same time receiving certain other
							special grants of power, and retaining the <foreign xml:lang="lat">
							imperium</foreign>, which he had now held uninterruptedly for twelve
							years. Hence the exaggerated expression <foreign xml:lang="lat">imperator
							unus</foreign>; for in the original sense of this title (see note on <ref n="pl252.6">p. 252, l. 6</ref>) it could be borne by as many
							officers as was necessary. It was not until the spring of <date when="-0045">B.C. 45</date>, some months after the delivery of this
							oration, that <foreign xml:lang="lat">Imperator</foreign> became the
							title of a new magistrate in whom the <foreign xml:lang="lat">imperium</foreign>
							was vested for his life, to be transmitted to his descendants. This was
							the commencement of the Empire, though the office was suspended from the
							death of Caesar till it was revived by Augustus. From this time the old
							use of this title was rare. <milestone n="228.15" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="alterum_7">, <gloss>
							second</gloss>. Cicero was <foreign xml:lang="lat">imperator</foreign>
							by virtue of his provincial government in Cilicia. <milestone n="228.17" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fascis_laureatos_7">:
							the <foreign xml:lang="lat">fasces</foreign> were wreathed with laurel
							when the commander, after a victory, was greeted as <foreign xml:lang="lat">imperator</foreign>. Cicero had aspired to the honor
							of a triumph for successes over some mountaineers in Cilicia, and
							therefore had not laid down his <foreign xml:lang="lat">imperium</foreign>
							at the time here referred to. <milestone n="228.18" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="reddere_7">, <gloss>
							restore</gloss>. This infin. represents a <foreign xml:lang="lat">
							conative</foreign> present having a future force; hence <foreign xml:lang="lat">dedisset</foreign> for fut. perf. of dir. disc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_8">, <gloss>
							how</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fuisse_9">,
							subject of <foreign xml:lang="lat">esse</foreign>. <milestone n="228.28" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nempe_9">,
							etc., <gloss>why! one who</gloss>, etc. <milestone n="228.31" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_Pharsalica_9">:
							the decisive victory of Caesar over Pompey, at Pharsalus in Thessaly,
							was gained August 9, <date when="-0048">B.C. 48</date>. <milestone n="228.32" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="petebat_9">, <gloss>aimed
							at</gloss> - <foreign xml:lang="lat">qui sensus</foreign>, <gloss>what
							were the sentiments</gloss>, etc.? A rhetorical way of asking him with
							which party he fought. <milestone n="229.2" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="optabas_9">, <gloss>pray
							for</gloss> (stronger than <foreign xml:lang="lat">cupiebas</foreign>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hic_10">,
							i.e. Caesar. <milestone n="229.13" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_vis_10">
							, <gloss>as you will have it</gloss>
							</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dicam_11"> = <foreign xml:lang="lat">dicturas sum</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eum_dictatorem_12">,
							i.e. Sulla, who, as dictator, had had full judicial powers. <milestone n="229.27" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="praemus_invitabat_12">:
							see note, Rosc. Am., sect. <ref n="Cic. S. Rosc. 6">6</ref> (<ref n="pl4.6">p. 4, l. 6</ref>). <milestone n="229.28" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="aliquot_post_12">
							, <gloss>some years later</gloss>. Sulla had provided by law for the
							impunity of those who executed his proscriptions; but Caesar, <gloss>as <foreign xml:lang="lat">judex quaestionis de sicariis</foreign>
								</gloss>
							, <date when="-0064">B.C. 64</date>, took pains to secure the trial and
							conviction of more than one of these bloodhounds. <milestone n="230.3" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="generis_familiae_12">,
							subjective gen.; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="virtutis_12">,
							objective gen. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="per_te_15">,
							i.e. as contrasted with the bloodthirstiness of some of his followers. <milestone n="230.26" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="essent_15">:
							the prot. is <foreign xml:lang="lat">si</foreign> . . . <foreign xml:lang="lat">esset</foreign>, above. <milestone n="230.27" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="reperiantur_15">:
							the seq. of tenses is violated to avoid ambiguity; § <ref n="AG 485">
							485</ref>, i (287, h, N.); B. 268, 7; G. 509, I, N.; II. 543 (491); cf.
							H.-B. 478; 50 <foreign xml:lang="lat">nolint</foreign> in 1.29. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="alicujus_16">,
							for <gloss>any one</gloss>. <milestone n="231.7" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="diceres_16">:
							the prot. is implied in </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tunc_16">
							(i.e. "if you were guarding Caesar against being deceived"); § <ref n="AG 521">521</ref>, a (310, a); B. 305, I G. 594, 3; 11.575, 9
							(507, N.7); H-B. 578,6. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="aditus_17">, <gloss>
							approach</gloss> (i.e. to Caesar in this case). <milestone n="231.13" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="velle_17">,
							etc., indir. disc. depending on the general idea of saying contained in
							the preceding sentence. <milestone n="231.14" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="de_aho_17">,
							etc.: i.e. first, why he selected Ligarius out of all Pompey's
							followers; second, how one who had committed precisely the same fault
							could have the audacity to bring the charge; and third, what new crime
							he had to accuse him of. The third point is expressed in the form of an
							indir. question; the other two are given as causes of the surprise. <milestone n="231.18" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qui_durius_17">
							(sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">appellant</foreign>), <gloss>those who
							speak more harshly</gloss>. <pb n="478"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fuerint_18">:
							§ <ref n="AG 440">440</ref> (266, c); cf. B. 308; G. 264; H. 559, 3
							(484, iii); H.-B. 532, I. <milestone n="231.30" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="contumeliam_18">:
							Cicero describes as <foreign xml:lang="lat">contumelia</foreign> the
							efforts of Pompey and the Senate to check the growing power of Caesar. <milestone n="231.32" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pacem_cupiebas_18">:
							it seems certain that Caesar had, in his desire for peace, carried his
							offers of compromise as far as it was possible for him to do safely in
							his position. <milestone n="232.1" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_conveniret_18">
							(in appos. with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="id_18">), <gloss>that
							you should come to an understanding</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="esses_19">,
							i.e. in the case supposed. <milestone n="232.8" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="secessionem_19">:
							Pompey and most of the Senate had retired at Caesar's approach to the
							city, and escaped to Greece. <milestone n="232.8" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="utrisque_cupientibus_19">
							, <gloss>where both wished</gloss>. <milestone n="232.11" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eorum_sequebantur_19">:
							almost the entire body of the Roman nobility followed Pompey. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nostram_20">,
							i.e. mine and my client's. <milestone n="232.19" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="poteramusne_20">,
							Sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">non venire</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Tuberonis_sors_21">:
							in the assignment of the provinces. <milestone n="232.28" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="excusare_21">
							, <gloss>to excuse himself</gloss>. <milestone n="232.30" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="contubernales_21">:
							in Cicero's brief campaign in the Social War. <milestone n="233.1" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quidam_21">, <gloss>some
							friend</gloss>: it is uncertain who. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="amplissimi_viri_22">,
							i.e. Pompey. <milestone n="233.6" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="occupatam_22">,
							i.e. by Attius Varus oil behalf of Pompey. <milestone n="233.8" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="voluisse_22">
							, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="voluisse_22_2">
							, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="maluisse_22">,
							all have the clause </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Africam_obtinere_22">
							depending on them, but it is expressed only with the second. <milestone n="233.9" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="natam_bellum_22">:
							a map of the Mediterranean will show the formidable position of the
							province of Africa as against Italy. <milestone n="233.11" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="aliquem_22">, <gloss>some
							one else</gloss> (subject of <foreign xml:lang="lat">maluisse</foreign>
							). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="querella_23">,
							i.e. " <foreign xml:lang="lat">recepti</foreign>... <foreign xml:lang="lat">sumus</foreign>," quoted directly. <milestone n="233.15" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="essetis_23">,
							Sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">recepti</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tradituri_fuistis_23">
							, <gloss>were you going to surrender?</gloss> Essentially equiv. to <foreign xml:lang="lat">tradidissetis</foreign>: § <ref n="AG 517">517</ref>,
							d (308, d); B. 304., 3, b; G. 597, R8, a; H. 583 (51 I, 2); H-B. 581, a. <milestone n="233.19" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sOrs_23">:
							Cf. Verr. 1, sect. <ref n="Cic. Ver. 1.1.21">21</ref>, and note
							(p.361.5). <milestone n="233.20" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cujus_interfuit_23">
							, <gloss>for whose interest it was</gloss>. <milestone n="233.21" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_probata_23">,
							as being an act of treachery which Caesar was too noble to approve. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="veniebatis_24">
							, <foreign xml:lang="lat">conative</foreign> imperfect. <milestone n="233.26" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="maxime_infestam_24">:
							King Juba of Numidia was a zealous adherent of Pompey, and Africa was
							the seat of the last struggle of the Senate against Caesar. <milestone n="233.27" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="huic_causae_24">,
							i.e. Caesar's. <milestone n="233.28" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="aliena_voluntas_24">
							, <gloss>estranged feeling</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="conventus_24">:
							an association of the citizens of a province, possessing certain
							corporate powers. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nempe_25">, <gloss>naturally
							enough</gloss>. <milestone n="234.2" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_societatem_25">
							, <gloss>to take a share in</gloss>. <milestone n="234.4" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="venissetis_25">
							, <gloss>you should have come</gloss> (not apod. but hortatory); <foreign xml:lang="lat">venistis</foreign> (emphatic), <gloss>you did come</gloss>
							.

<milestone n="234.7" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="per_me_25">, <gloss>for
							all me</gloss>. <milestone n="234.10" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qui_25">
							(causal) <foreign xml:lang="lat">privaverit</foreign>, <gloss>in that he
							deprived you</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quamvis_probarem_26">
							, <gloss>however much I might approve:</gloss>
								<foreign xml:lang="lat">
							probarem</foreign> is used instead of <foreign xml:lang="lat">probem</foreign>
							on account of the tense of <foreign xml:lang="lat">commemorarem</foreign>
							(cont. to fact). That <foreign xml:lang="lat">probarem</foreign> itself
							is not cont. to fact is shown by <foreign xml:lang="lat">ut probo</foreign>
							.

<milestone n="234.17" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quotus_quisque_26">
							, <gloss>how many</gloss> (see Vocab.). <milestone n="234.18" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="partibus_26">
							, <gloss>party</gloss>. <milestone n="234.19" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="crudelitate_26">:
							because the younger Tubero was sick at the time and needed to be put on
							shore. <milestone n="234.20" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ad_ipsos_26">,
							construed with <foreign xml:lang="lat">partibus</foreign>: § <ref n="AG 280">280</ref>, a (182, a); B. 235, B, 2, c; G. 211, R.1; H.
							389, 1 (636, iv, 4); H.-B. 325, 631, 6. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_fuissent_27">:
							§ <ref n="AG 440">440</ref> (266, c); Cf. B. 308; G. 608; H. 586, ii
							(515, iii) ; H.-B. 532, 2, b. <milestone n="234.24" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nequaquam_fuerunt_27">:
							Varus was of an insignificant family, while the Tuberos were members of
							the nobility. <milestone n="234.25" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="justo_27">, <gloss>
							regular</gloss> (duly conferred). <milestone n="234.27" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ad_Caesarem_27">,
							Sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">venit</foreign>
								<milestone n="234.28" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="causam_27">, <gloss>
							side</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ejus_28">,
							i.e. Pompey's. <milestone n="235.2" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="bellis_28">:
							there is a gap here, which must have contained a thought like "Was there
							in your minds a zealous desire of victory?" <pb n="480"/>
								<milestone n="235.5" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_videres_28">,
							second person of indef. subject in a general condition: § <ref n="AG 518">518</ref>, a (309, a); cf. B. 302, 2; cf. G. 597, R3; H.
							578, 2 (508 5); cf. H.-B. 504, 2. <milestone n="235.7" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="esset_28">,
							subj. of characteristic; but for that it would be indic. (<foreign xml:lang="lat">erat</foreign>) by § <ref n="AG 517">517</ref>, b
							(308, b) ; G. 599, a.3; 14.583 (511, N3); H-B. 581, e. <milestone n="235.8" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vicisses_28">,
							integral part (for fut. perf. <foreign xml:lang="lat">viceris</foreign>
							). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_causa_29">,
							i.e. in upholding the side of Pompey. <milestone n="235.17" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ad_summam_29">
							, <gloss>to one main point</gloss>
								<milestone n="235.19" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="equidem_29">
							emphasizes </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="multas_29">. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tecum_30">, <gloss>in
							company with you</gloss>. Caesar was hardly less distinguished as an
							orator than as a general and statesman. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dum_tenuit_30">:
							for tense, see § <ref n="AG 556">556</ref> and a (276, e and N.); B.
							293, i; G. 569, N.1; H. 533, 4 (467, 4, N.); H.-B. 550, b-ratio <foreign xml:lang="lat">honorum</foreign>
								<foreign xml:lang="lat">tuorum</foreign>
							, <gloss>the course of ambition</gloss> (lit. <gloss>the consideration
							of your</gloss> [series. of] <gloss>offices</gloss>). The regular course
							of a Roman's ambition led him through the <foreign xml:lang="lat">cursus
							honorum</foreign>, i.e. from the quaestorship to the consulship. One of
							the chief means of advancing his political interests in this career was
							to act as advocate (<foreign xml:lang="lat">patronus</foreign>) in the
							Forum. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_foro_30">:
							the Forum was the seat of the administration of justice. <milestone n="235.22" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="posthac_30">,
							Sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">fecerit</foreign>
								<milestone n="235.24" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dic_30"> and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quaere_30">
							in effect form a <foreign xml:lang="lat">protasis</foreign> of which <foreign xml:lang="lat">taceo</foreign> is the <foreign xml:lang="lat">
							apodosis</foreign>,- if you <gloss>say</gloss>, etc., <gloss>I am silent</gloss>:
							§ <ref n="AG 521">521</ref>, b (310, b); B. 305, 2; G. 593, 4; H. 573,
							i (507, I); H.-B. 497, 2. <milestone n="235.25" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quibus_praesidus_30">
							, <gloss>in which army</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ne_quidem_30">,
							i.e. the following. <milestone n="235.26" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="valerent_30">
							, <gloss>might prevail</gloss> (if I used them). <milestone n="235.27" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="bello_oppressus_30">
							, <gloss>overtaken by the war</gloss>. <milestone n="235.28" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_ipso_30">,
							i.e. in his conduct in the war to which he was forced. <milestone n="235.30" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="temere_30">, <gloss>
							thoughtlessly</gloss>. <milestone n="235.31" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ignoscatur_30">,
							impersonal. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="impetravit_30">,
							Sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">veniam</foreign>. <milestone n="235.32" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="adroganter_30">,
							Sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">oro</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="idem_qui_30">
							, <gloss>as you have</gloss>, etc. (lit. <gloss>the same one who have</gloss>
							). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="mihi_31">,
							etc., i.e. I have been not only spared myself but am allowed to appear
							for another. <milestone n="236.3" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="studus_31">, <gloss>zealous
							efforts</gloss>. The thought is that Caesar is accustomed to decide such
							cases not with reference to any pleading or any wish to gratify his own
							friends but rather with reference to the character of the petitioners
							and their relation to the defendant. <milestone n="236.6" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="causas_31">, <gloss>the
							cause</gloss>. <pb n="481"/>
								<milestone n="236.7" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="voltus_31">,
							the tears and lamentations by which it was customary to seek acquittal
							(see peroration of Defence of Milo). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quam_necessarius_31">
							, <gloss>how closely connected with you</gloss>. <milestone n="236.8" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quam_filius_31">,
							opposed to <foreign xml:lang="lat">tuus</foreign>. <milestone n="236.10" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fruuntur_31">
							, <foreign xml:lang="lat">concedas</foreign>: the indic. refers to
							individual cases; the subj. characterizes Caesar himself, but the
							difference is slight. <milestone n="236.13" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="justissimum_31">
							, <gloss>best founded</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tu_32">, not
							expressed for emphasis but merely to carry the concessive <foreign xml:lang="lat">quidem</foreign>. <milestone n="236.17" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Sabinos_32">:
							Ligarius was of Sabine origin, and many of his Sabine friends were
							present on this occasion. <milestone n="236.18" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fiorem_32">,
							etc.: the Sabine territory among the mountains was still the home of a
							hardy and virtuous population. <milestone n="236.19" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nosti_32">:
							during the First Civil War, Caesar had found shelter from Sulla among
							these kindly mountaineers. <milestone n="236.21" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="squalorem_32">:
							it was the custom of the Romans to express their sympathy for one in
							danger by going into mourning, that is, by wearing ragged and mean
							apparel. When Cicero was threatened with exile some 20,000 of his
							friends are said to have appeared in this guise. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quodvis_33">, <gloss>any
							whatever</gloss> (emphatic). <milestone n="236.28" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vox_33">, the
							expression which follows. <milestone n="236.29" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vicit_33">,
							i.e. it was this sentiment of Caesar, as opposed to the bloodthirstiness
							of the Pompeians, that won him the victory in the Civil War. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nos_33">,
							i.e. the party of Pompey. <milestone n="236.30" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nisi_qui_33">
							, <gloss>except those who</gloss>. <milestone n="237.4" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="veste_mutata_33">
							, <gloss>in mourning</gloss> (see note on <ref n="pl236.21">p. 236, l.
							21</ref>, above). <milestone n="237.5" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tecum_fuerunt_33">
							, <gloss>on your side</gloss>, i.e. as holding aloof from the other
							side. Being neutrals, they had been threatened by the Pompeians. <milestone n="237.6" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_nulli_33">
							, <gloss>some of us</gloss>. <milestone n="237.7" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tuis_suos_33">
							, <gloss>to your friends their friends</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fuerit_futurus_34">:
							see note on <ref n="pl233.15">p. 233, l. 15</ref>. <milestone n="237.13" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="conspirantem_34">
							, <gloss>harmonious</gloss> (breathing together); <foreign xml:lang="lat">conflatum</foreign>, <gloss>identical</gloss> (fused
							together). <milestone n="237.15" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quidvis_ut_34">
							, <gloss>that</gloss> ANYTHING <gloss>would have happened before these
							brothers</gloss>, etc. <milestone n="237.16" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_sequerentur_34">,
							subst. clause of result: § <ref n="AG 571">571</ref>, a (332, b); B.
							284, 4; G. 557, N.2; H. 570, I (502, 2); cf. H.-B. 507, 4, d. <pb n="482"/>
								<milestone n="237.17" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tempestate_34">
							, <gloss>by stress of weather</gloss>. <milestone n="237.19" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tamen_34">, <gloss>in
							spite of that</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="jerit_35">,
							etc. (concessive <foreign xml:lang="lat">subi</foreign>.), <gloss>suppose
							he did go</gloss>. <milestone n="237.21" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hi_tui_35">
							, <gloss>
									<emph>these</emph>entreat you and they are yours</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="equidem_35">
							sets off the implied subject <foreign xml:lang="lat">ego</foreign>
							against <foreign xml:lang="lat">tu</foreign>, below. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_interessem_35">
							, <gloss>having been concerned in</gloss>. <milestone n="237.22" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quaestor_urbanus_35">
							, <gloss>city treasurer</gloss> (see Introd., p. lix), in which capacity
							he appears to have done a service to Caesar, who was then in Gaul. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nihil_aliud_36">
							, <gloss>had no other aim</gloss>. <milestone n="237.29" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="haec_36">, <gloss>the
							present condition of things</gloss>, i.e. T. Ligarius could not have any
							interested motive in doing this favor, since he could not foresee how
							powerful Caesar was to become. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eum_36">: § <ref n="AG 300">300</ref>, b (196, a, 2, N.); G. 521, N.3; H. 449, II. <milestone n="237.31" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="officio_36">, <gloss>
							service</gloss> (to you). <milestone n="237.32" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tot_talibus_36">
							, <gloss>many and excellent as they are</gloss>. <milestone n="238.2" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="condonaveris_36">
							: <gloss>
									<foreign xml:lang="lat">condonare</foreign>
								</gloss>
							is to grant something for the sake of some one else. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="de_nobilissimo_37">,
							i.e. Marcellus. <milestone n="238.4" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_curia_37">,
							before the Senate (see Introd. to Oration for Marcellus). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="foro_37">:
							Ligarius had been accused; hence the form of trial in the Forum. <milestone n="238.10" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="populare_37">
							, <gloss>popular</gloss>, but in a strictly political sense. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_possis_38">:
							a subst. clause of result (see note on p.237,l. 16), because an effect
							is implied in <foreign xml:lang="lat">habet</foreign>. <milestone n="238.16" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="postulet_38">:
							§ <ref n="AG 447">447</ref>, a (311, a, N3); G. 459, R.; H. 552 (485);
							Cf. H-B. 517, I. <milestone n="238.18" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tantum_38">, <gloss>so
							much only</gloss> (as often). </gloss>
